title: How to Work with Grief That Surfaces During Psychedelic Journeys
body:
How to Work with Grief That Surfaces During Psychedelic Journeys

1. Opening Hook - Personal Anecdote

I remember the first time grief hit me hard during a psychedelic journey. It wasn’t the joyous epiphany I’d been expecting. Instead, it felt like a tidal wave, washing over me with a heaviness I couldn’t escape. I was sitting in a quiet room, soft music playing, and suddenly, memories of my mother flooded my mind - memories I hadn’t touched in years. The sadness was raw and unexpected, a deep ache that wasn’t just sadness but a complex knot of loss, love, regret, and longing. It wasn’t something I could shut off or push aside; it demanded my attention.

For years, I wrestled with what to do when grief surfaced mid-journey. Should I fight it? Embrace it? Run from it? Over time, I learned that grief is both a teacher and a doorway. Psychedelics can crack open these doors in unexpected ways, revealing emotions and memories we often keep locked away. But how we handle these moments shapes the healing we get from them.

If you’re here because grief has shown up during your psychedelic work, you’re not alone. Many of us have faced this shadow side of the journey. In this article, I’ll share what I’ve learned from my own experiences, scientific research, and practical tools for working with grief during and after these sessions. The goal isn’t just to survive the wave, but to find meaning and, eventually, peace on the other side.

2. What Is Happening in the Brain When Grief Surfaces During Psychedelic Sessions

Grief is more than just sadness; it’s a complex emotional and neurobiological state that involves several brain regions working together. When grief surfaces during a psychedelic experience, it often reactivates neural pathways linked to memory, attachment, and emotional processing.

Psychedelics like psilocybin temporarily alter the function of the brain’s default mode network (DMN), a hub responsible for self-referential thought and the narrative we tell ourselves about our identity. Research from Robin Carhart-Harris and his team at Imperial College London has shown that psychedelics reduce activity and connectivity in the DMN, allowing for a loosening of rigid mental patterns. This flexibility can enable repressed or stuck emotions - like grief - to emerge into conscious awareness.

At the same time, psychedelics enhance communication between brain regions that don’t usually “talk” to each other. This heightened connectivity can bring memories and feelings from the limbic system (the emotional center) up to the surface, sometimes bypassing the usual filters of the prefrontal cortex. That means grief that’s been buried beneath layers of denial or avoidance may suddenly feel immediate and visceral.

There’s also the role of serotonin receptors, specifically the 5-HT2A receptor, which psychedelics activate. This receptor is heavily involved in mood regulation and emotional processing. By stimulating these receptors, psychedelics can intensify emotional experiences, sometimes amplifying grief but also opening a path to working through it rather than suppressing it.

From a psychological perspective, grief during psychedelic sessions can be a form of catharsis - a chance to process loss in a way that is difficult in ordinary consciousness. Stanislav Grof, one of the pioneers of psychedelic therapy, called this “perinatal matrices,” where unresolved trauma and emotional pain are revisited during altered states. While grief isn’t limited to perinatal trauma, this concept highlights how psychedelics allow deep emotional material to emerge for healing.

Understanding these brain changes helps us see why grief during a journey isn’t a malfunction or something to fear. Instead, it’s the mind’s way of trying to process and integrate painful emotions that have been sidelined by everyday distractions and defense mechanisms.

3. Research and Evidence: Studies That Shed Light on Psychedelic-Assisted Grief Work

Scientific research on psychedelics and grief is still emerging, but the results so far are promising and illuminating. One of the most relevant studies comes from Charles Raison and colleagues, who explored psilocybin-assisted therapy for alleviating death anxiety and grief in patients with life-threatening cancer diagnoses. Their 2016 study showed that a single session of psilocybin significantly reduced anxiety and depression, and participants reported deep emotional breakthroughs related to their impending loss.

Similarly, a landmark trial led by Roland Griffiths at Johns Hopkins demonstrated that psilocybin can produce long-lasting reductions in depression and anxiety, often linked to unresolved grief or trauma. Participants frequently described encounters with lost loved ones and a reevaluation of their relationship to death and loss. Griffiths’ work is crucial because it validates the therapeutic potential of psychedelic experiences when grief surfaces, showing that these moments can lead to deep healing rather than harm.

Another notable research thread is the use of MDMA-assisted therapy for grief and PTSD. While MDMA isn’t a classic psychedelic, it similarly enhances emotional openness and reduces fear responses, allowing people to confront painful memories safely. Studies by Michael Mithoefer and others have found that this approach helps patients work through traumatic grief by fostering empathy and connection, reducing avoidance patterns.

The work of psychiatrist and ethnobotanist Paul Stamets, though more focused on mushroom biology, also stresses the importance of the “set and setting” in psychedelic therapy. He points out that grief, when it arises, must be held in a safe and supportive context to guide the emotional process constructively. This is echoed in clinical practice, where therapists trained in psychedelic integration provide containment and guidance to help patients move through overwhelming grief.

Michael Pollan’s How to Change Your Mind also offers vivid accounts of people encountering grief during sessions and how these encounters ultimately helped them find acceptance and a deeper sense of meaning. The narrative Pollan presents aligns with clinical findings, highlighting that grief is a natural and often necessary part of the psychedelic healing process.

4. Practical Steps for Working with Grief That Arises on Psychedelic Journeys

Encountering grief mid-journey can feel like an emotional storm, but there are ways to work with it constructively rather than getting swept away. First, patience is key. When grief emerges, resist the urge to fight it or distract yourself. Instead, try to lean into the feeling as gently as you can. Accepting grief doesn’t mean wallowing; it means acknowledging what’s present without judgment.

One practical tool I recommend is deliberate breathwork. Slow, deep breaths can help regulate your nervous system and keep you grounded during intense emotional waves. I’ve found that simply counting breaths or focusing on the sensation of air entering and leaving the body can prevent grief feelings from becoming overwhelming. This is something you can practice before the journey to build confidence.

Another useful technique is journaling. Bringing a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound into your integration space allows you to capture insights and emotions as they arise. Writing about grief after the session helps process and contextualize the experience, turning ephemeral feelings into tangible reflections. Journaling can also act as a safe container where you can explore difficult emotions on your own terms.

During the journey, if you feel safe, gently exploring the memories or images tied to your grief can be illuminating. Ask yourself, “What is this grief trying to tell me? What part of me is hurting?” Sometimes grief hides messages about unmet needs or unresolved conflicts. Naming these can bring clarity and open doors to healing.

After the session, consider sharing your experience with a trusted guide or therapist trained in psychedelic integration. Talking through grief with someone skilled in navigating these waters can provide emotional support and practical strategies. Therapists who understand the neurobiology and psychology of psychedelic grief work can help prevent feelings of isolation and despair that sometimes crop up.

Lastly, don’t underestimate the power of physical care. Grief can be exhausting. Rest, hydration, nutrition, and gentle movement like yoga or walking can help your body metabolize emotional energy. Bessel van der Kolk’s The Body Keeps the Score is a great resource on how trauma and grief are held in the body, and practical ways to support healing through somatic practices.

5. Common Challenges When Working with Grief on Psychedelic Journeys - and How to Overcome Them

Not every encounter with grief during a psychedelic session is smooth sailing. There are common pitfalls that many face, and knowing these can prepare you to handle them better. One major challenge is feeling overwhelmed or trapped by grief. The intensity can trigger panic or despair, making it hard to stay present and process the emotion constructively.

If you find yourself spiraling into panic, grounding techniques are vital. Remind yourself that the experience is temporary and your nervous system will settle. Focus on sensory anchors like feeling the floor beneath you or listening to gentle music. Sometimes simply opening your eyes and looking around can interrupt the overwhelming loop.

Another challenge is resistance - trying to shove grief away or numb it with distractions. This can prolong emotional suffering and even deepen trauma in the long run. Instead, practice compassionate curiosity. Ask, “What am I resisting? What fear lies beneath this pushback?” Often, resistance is rooted in fear of pain, but grief needs space to breathe to heal.

Some people experience confusion during grief-related journeys. Emotional or symbolic experiences may feel fragmented or puzzling. This can be frightening because it challenges our sense of reality and meaning. Keeping a journal handy during integration helps organize these fragments over time, turning them into a coherent narrative.

A lack of support is another common issue. Grief can isolate us, and without proper guidance, the psychedelic experience may feel destabilizing. Building a support network before diving into these journeys - whether friends, therapists, or community groups - creates a safety net. Knowing someone will listen and hold space after your session can make all the difference.

Finally, expect that grief work is not linear. Progress may come in fits and starts, with days of breakthrough followed by setbacks. Patience and self-compassion are essential. Think of this process like tending a garden; some seeds take time to sprout, and the soil must be nurtured gently, day by day.

6. Integration of Psychedelic Grief Work into Daily Life for Long-Term Healing

Grief that surfaces during a psychedelic session doesn’t end when the effects wear off. The real work often begins afterward, as you bring insights and emotions back into your daily reality. Integration is the bridge between the raw experience and lasting change, and it requires ongoing attention.

One of the best ways I’ve found to integrate grief work is through routine reflective practices like journaling or meditation. Writing in a Journaling Notebook Leather Bound after a session keeps the dialogue open with your inner self. Meditation can help you observe grief without becoming overwhelmed, noticing its ebb and flow over time.

Physical practices remain crucial too. Grief often manifests in bodily tension or discomfort. Regular movement, breathwork, or somatic therapies like those described in The Body Keeps the Score can help release stuck energy and support emotional processing beyond the psychedelic session.

It’s also helpful to create rituals that honor your grief - a way to keep your connection with loss alive but framed within a context of healing. Lighting a candle on important dates, visiting a place that reminds you of a lost loved one, or even planting a tree can provide grounding and meaning.

Working with a therapist or counselor who understands psychedelic integration can amplify the benefits. They can help you identify patterns, reframe limiting beliefs, and develop coping strategies that make grief less isolating. Integration groups, either in person or online, also offer community, reminding you you’re not alone in this journey.

Above all, give yourself permission to feel grief as a natural and ongoing part of life. The psychedelic experience often opens doors to deeper emotional honesty, but long-term healing happens through consistent, compassionate attention. By weaving grief work into your everyday habits, you turn fleeting psychedelic insights into lasting transformation.

7. Closing Reflection: Sitting with Grief as a Portal, Not a Prison

When grief surfaces during a psychedelic journey, it can feel like being stuck in a dark room with no clear exit. Yet, from my years of experience, grief is less a prison and more a portal - a gateway to deeper understanding and connection. It demands bravery to face it, but the invitation it offers is deep.

Grief exposes what we hold most dear. It reminds us of love that doesn’t disappear, even when loss feels unbearable. Psychedelic journeys often strip away distractions, leaving us raw and vulnerable. This vulnerability is uncomfortable but also sacred. It’s in this space that real healing takes root.

One of my favorite reminders comes from the work of grief counselor Alan Wolfelt, who says grief is not a problem to fix but a process to live through. Psychedelics accelerate this process by bringing emotions upfront, but they don’t eliminate grief altogether. Instead, they ask us to meet it fully and honestly.

As you contemplate your own experiences with grief during psychedelic sessions, consider how you might be carrying grief that hasn’t yet been named or felt. What parts of your story need space to breathe? How can you move toward those feelings with kindness instead of fear?

The journey with grief is lifelong. Psychedelic medicine can shed light on hidden corners, but walking through those illuminated halls requires courage, patience, and sometimes, companionship. Remember that grief and love live side by side, and embracing one deepens your capacity for the other.

So next time grief visits on a journey or in daily life, instead of turning away, try sitting with it for a moment. Breathe into it. Listen closely. You might find that grief, with all its weight and tenderness, is one of the truest teachers we have.
